model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model that preserves the complaint-derived resolution (muscle tension in shoulders/jaw with headaches, anticipatory threat, and relaxation difficulty) while using a compact, modifiable predictor set. Six criteria (C01–C06) capture current muscle tension, interoceptive awareness, tension headaches, persistent threat anticipation, relaxation difficulty on low-stress days, and habitual bracing. Six predictors (P01–P06) target practical salutogenic levers: progressive muscle relaxation adherence, diaphragmatic breathing, interoceptive body-scan practice, sleep routine consistency, jaw-bruxism monitoring, and mindfulness-based self-regulation. Measurements rely on brief 1–9 Likert EMA items for select criteria (4 prompts/day) and discrete, safe behavioral indicators for predictors; one passive sensor (jaw clench) complements the plan. This configuration emphasizes within-person variance, minimizes redundancy, and supports a principled gVAR calibration using a 14-day window with 4 momentary prompts/day and daily diary data.
variable_selection_notes: Selected six non-redundant, clinically plausible criteria anchored in C01–C06 to cover muscle tension, awareness, headaches, anticipatory threat, arousal regulation, and habitual bracing. Predictors were chosen for feasibility, safety, and likely causal pathways aligned with the HyDe and LLM mappings (PMR, breathwork, interoception, sleep, bruxism sensing, mindfulness). All measures favor 1–9 Likert or continuous signals to maximize within-person variance; predictors include one passive signal (jaw bruxism) and several brief self-reports, reducing multicollinearity risk. Clear documentation of data collection modes enables replicable gVAR modeling; acknowledge potential nonlinearity in some predictor effects (e.g., sleep and mindfulness in relation to threat) which is addressed via the edges and explicit sign spec.

momentary_ema.notes: 4 prompts/day during waking hours with slight jitter to avoid clock-like responses.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary to capture sleep, stress context, and arousal context.

passive_streams.notes: Jaw clench/bruxism sensor (wearable) passively contributes to C01/C03/C06 in daily aggregates.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Moderate-to-strong feasibility with 14 days and 56 EMA prompts; monitor adherence and missingness.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Expect substantial within-person variance for C01–C06 across days and blocks; plan for per-person z-scoring or mixed-effects centering.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Implement engagement reminders, device checks, and small participant compensation to mitigate data gaps.

safety_notes: This is an observational/data-collection plan. No diagnosis or medication recommendations are provided. If acute risk emerges in any data stream, escalate to appropriate clinical care.

source_variable.label: Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your current shoulder and jaw muscle tension on a 1–9 scale (1 = no tension, 9 = maximum tension).

criteria.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension
criteria.query_text_used: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension | Operational item/signal: Right now, how aware are you of your bodily tension on a 1–9 scale? (1 = not at all aware, 9 = highly aware). | Polarity: higher_is_better

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to moment-to-moment self-report of awareness of bodily tension. The leaf 'body awareness' (NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / physical / physical self-care / listening to body signals / body awareness) explicitly corresponds to monitoring bodily states via EMA self-report on a 1–9 scale, aligning with the measurement item and mode (momentary, smartphone EMA). Among candidates, it is the most specific and directly tied to bodily tension awareness in the physiological domain.

source_variable.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how aware are you of your bodily tension on a 1–9 scale? (1 = not at all aware, 9 = highly aware).

criteria.label: Headaches triggered by muscular tension
criteria.query_text_used: Headaches triggered by muscular tension | Operational item/signal: Rate current headache intensity on a 1–9 scale (1 = no headache, 9 = severe headache). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable C03 represents headaches triggered by muscular tension. The best leaf is the tension-type headache – mild_to_moderate_intensity (idx 16165), a primary headache disorder commonly linked to neck/shoulder muscle tension. Measurement mode EMA_self_report with 1–9 headaches intensity fits the item: rate current headache intensity (1–9). This provides a specific operational construct matching the variable's label, measurement item, and mechanism (muscular tension contributing to TTH) while staying consistent with the pseudoprofile context.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate current headache intensity on a 1–9 scale (1 = no headache, 9 = severe headache).

criteria.label: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation
criteria.query_text_used: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation | Operational item/signal: Right now, how strong is the sense that something bad might happen? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely likely). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 maps to Persistent anticipatory threat expectation. Measurement is EMA self-report on a 1–9 scale (Right now, how strong is the sense that something bad might happen?). The closest ontology leaf is 30619: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / bipolar and related disorders / bipolar ii disorder / specifiers / with anxious distress / fear_that_something_awful_may_happen, which encodes anticipatory threat/fear of an upcoming negative event. This leaf provides the most specific operational construct for persistent anticipatory threat within a clinical anxiety/affectivity context and aligns with the pseudoprofile’s emphasis on anticipatory threat and arousal regulation. Other leaves (anticipatory anxiety variants) are more situational or different phobia/anxiety domains, whereas fear_that_something_awful_may_happen directly mirrors the measurement item’s content.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how strong is the sense that something bad might happen? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely likely).

criteria.label: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days | Operational item/signal: At this moment, how difficult is it to relax given the current (low-stress) context? (1 = very easy, 9 = very difficult). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable C05 (Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days) is an EMA self-report of a cognitive relaxation state. The most specific operational leaf is mental relaxation (NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / emotional / positive emotions / contentment / relaxation / *mental relaxation), which directly captures the momentary ease or difficulty of achieving a relaxed mental state in the reported context.

measurement.item_or_signal: At this moment, how difficult is it to relax given the current (low-stress) context? (1 = very easy, 9 = very difficult).

criteria.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat
criteria.query_text_used: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat | Operational item/signal: Right now, to what extent are you bracing/tensing posturally or facially? (1 = not at all, 9 = very strongly). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: C06 describes habitual anticipatory muscle bracing (postural/facial). The measurement is an EMA self-report on current bracing intensity (1-9) via smartphone, 4 prompts/day, recalling since last prompt. The closest ontology leaf among candidates is the generic muscle_tension node under generalized anxiety disorder with somatic symptoms (idx 32068), which explicitly represents muscle tension as a somatic symptom measured in momentary self-reports. Although there is no leaf that exactly captures 'habitual anticipatory bracing in absence of immediate threat,' selecting the muscle_tension leaf provides the most specific operational construct that matches the variable’s mechanism (muscle tension/brace-related arousal) and measurement modality. This aligns with the protocol’s aims (PMR, breathwork, interoception) that target muscle tension as a lever, and uses the same EMA/1-9 scale structure described for the data collection.

mapping.leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / anxiety disorders / generalized anxiety disorder / with predominant somatic symptoms / muscle_tension

source_variable.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, to what extent are you bracing/tensing posturally or facially? (1 = not at all, 9 = very strongly).

predictors.label: PMR_adherence
predictors.query_text_used: PMR_adherence | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: PMR reduces peripheral muscle tension and ungulates overall arousal via progressive muscle relaxation. | Intervention examples: Daily PMR session (e.g., 20 minutes) focusing on shoulders, jaw, neck. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Minutes spent practicing PMR since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice (idx 3053) which directly corresponds to PMR_adherence. Variable label PMR_adherence targets PMR practice; measurement item is minutes spent practicing PMR since last prompt, captured via EMA_self_report; the leaf path PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice precisely reflects the intended predictor. The measurement mode/item align (minutes, EMA, since_last_prompt) and the mechanism (PMR reduces muscle tension and arousal) support this mapping as the most specific operational construct.

source_variable.label: PMR_adherence
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: PMR reduces peripheral muscle tension and ungulates overall arousal via progressive muscle relaxation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Daily PMR session (e.g., 20 minutes) focusing on shoulders, jaw, neck.

measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes spent practicing PMR since last prompt

expected_effects.rationale: PMR reduces muscle tension which should lower C01 in the near term.
Relaxation reduces habitual bracing (C06) via reduced arousal.

feasibility.notes: Feasible with 56 EMA prompts + diary + passive data.

predictors.label: Breathing_practice
predictors.query_text_used: Breathing_practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Slow diaphragmatic breathing reduces sympathetic arousal and muscle tension. | Intervention examples: Daily 5–10 minutes of diaphragmatic breathing. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Minutes spent on diaphragmatic breathing since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *diaphragmatic_breathing_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is diaphragmatic_breathing_practice (PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *diaphragmatic_breathing_practice) because the variable P02 is Breathing_practice specifically focusing on diaphragmatic breathing. The measurement item is Minutes spent on diaphragmatic breathing since last prompt, captured via EMA_self_report on a smartphone app with 4 prompts per day (momentary assessment). This aligns with the diaphragmatic_breathing_practice leaf, which represents diaphragmatic breathing as a relaxation/physiological downshift skill. Mechanism rationale (slower diaphragmatic breathing reduces sympathetic arousal and muscle tension) supports this construct, and the measurement mode/item directly maps to the leaf.

source_variable.label: Breathing_practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Slow diaphragmatic breathing reduces sympathetic arousal and muscle tension.

measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes spent on diaphragmatic breathing since last prompt

expected_effects.rationale: Breathing down-regulates arousal, potentially lowering muscle tension (C01).
Better breathing may reduce headache intensity by reducing muscle tension.

feasibility.notes: Low-burden intervention with clear daily window.

predictors.label: Interoceptive_body_scan_training
predictors.query_text_used: Interoceptive_body_scan_training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Body scan enhances interoceptive accuracy and regulation of arousal; may improve C02 and C04. | Intervention examples: Brief daily body-scan (5–15 minutes). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Minutes spent on body scan training since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / body_based_mindfulness / *body_scan_with_breath_integration

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: body_scan_practice (PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / body_based_mindfulness / *body_scan_practice). The variable label Interoceptive_body_scan_training and the measurement item Minutes spent on body scan training since last prompt align directly with a body scan practice. EMA_self_report via smartphone captures minutes spent per prompt (4 prompts/day) with recall window since last prompt. Mechanism: body scan training enhances interoceptive accuracy and regulation of arousal, supporting interoceptive criteria (C02) and threat/arousal modulation (C04) within the pseudoprofile. This leaf is the most specific, directly matching the intended predictor among candidates; it is not overfit to a broader or breath-integrated variant.

source_variable.label: Interoceptive_body_scan_training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Body scan enhances interoceptive accuracy and regulation of arousal; may improve C02 and C04.

measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes spent on body scan training since last prompt

expected_effects.rationale: Better interoceptive awareness supports early tension detection and regulation.
Improved threat appraisal via mindful awareness may reduce anticipatory threat.

predictors.label: Sleep_routine_consistency
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep_routine_consistency | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Regular sleep routines stabilize circadian arousal, reducing daytime tension and anticipatory threat. | Intervention examples: Maintain consistent bedtime and wind-down routine. | Measurement: behavioral_log | Hours slept last night (0–12) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_routines_and_practices / presleep_downshift / *progressive_muscle_relaxation

mapping.rationale: P04 Sleep_routine_consistency predictor; variable measurement Hours slept last night (0–12) aligns with sleep timing/duration captured in a sleep diary. The closest and most specific operational leaf is BIO / medical_assessment_and_physiology_testing / circadian_and_sleep_tracking / sleep_diary, which is explicitly designed for end-of-day logs of sleep duration and timing. This matches the measurement mode (behavioral_log) and the intended use of sleep routine consistency data within a gVAR predictor set.

source_variable.label: Sleep_routine_consistency
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Regular sleep routines stabilize circadian arousal, reducing daytime tension and anticipatory threat.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Maintain consistent bedtime and wind-down routine.

measurement.item_or_signal: Hours slept last night (0–12)

expected_effects.rationale: Improved sleep tends to reduce daily threat anticipation and arousal.
Better sleep reduces daytime muscle tension load.

feasibility.notes: Low burden if sleep log integrated with daily diary.

predictors.label: Jaw_bruxism_sensor
predictors.query_text_used: Jaw_bruxism_sensor | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Jaw clenching/bruxism captured passively reflects peripheral muscle tension and arousal load, informing C01/C03/C06. | Intervention examples: N/A (passive sensor) | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Continuous jaw clench/bruxism signal (0-1) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / neurophysiology_and_stress_proxies / muscle_tension / *jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor

mapping.rationale: Selected the exact leaf for Jaw_bruxism_sensor: jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor under muscle_tension. This matches the variable label and measurement: passive_phone_sensor with a continuous 0-1 signal sampled 24 times/day. It directly represents peripheral jaw/muscle tension and arousal relevant to C01/C03/C06, making it the most specific operational construct among candidates. Other leaves refer to devices or interventions (e.g., mouthguards, warm compresses) or non-passive metrics, which are less aligned with a passive jaw bruxism sensor.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / neurophysiology_and_stress_proxies / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor

source_variable.label: Jaw_bruxism_sensor
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Jaw clenching/bruxism captured passively reflects peripheral muscle tension and arousal load, informing C01/C03/C06.

measurement.item_or_signal: Continuous jaw clench/bruxism signal (0-1)

expected_effects.rationale: Increased bruxism aligns with higher muscle tension in C01.
Bruxism associated with headaches risk/strength of headaches.
Bruxism may reflect habitual bracing (C06).

feasibility.notes: Requires wearable integration; ensure battery life and compliance.

predictors.label: Mindfulness_interoception_training
predictors.query_text_used: Mindfulness_interoception_training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Mindfulness and interoceptive training enhance present-moment regulation, potentially reducing threat sensitivity and improving relaxation. | Intervention examples: Brief pre-event centering, body scan, and mindful breathing (4–10 minutes). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Minutes spent on mindfulness/interoception practice since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / applied_mindfulness_for_stress_and_reactivity / *pre_event_centering_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf: interoceptive_awareness_practice under mindfulness-based interventions / body_based_mindfulness. The variable Mindfulness_interoception_training corresponds to minutes spent on mindfulness/interoception practice (EMA item). The measurement mode (EMA_self_report, minutes) aligns with this leaf. Mechanism: mindfulness and interoceptive training enhance present-moment regulation, potentially reducing threat sensitivity and improving relaxation, which fits the stated mechanism rationale.

source_variable.label: Mindfulness_interoception_training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Mindfulness and interoceptive training enhance present-moment regulation, potentially reducing threat sensitivity and improving relaxation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief pre-event centering, body scan, and mindful breathing (4–10 minutes).

measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes spent on mindfulness/interoception practice since last prompt

expected_effects.rationale: Mindfulness reduces threat reactivity and anticipatory arousal.
Improved interoceptive awareness supports proactive regulation.
Present-moment regulation may attenuate muscle tension.
Mindfulness may improve relaxation during low-stress contexts.

feasibility.notes: Combines brief EMA with existing passive data; low burden when integrated with activity data.
